title: Great place to grow as an engineer with strong workâlife balance
body: Great place to grow as an engineer with strong workâlife balance
pros: SAP is a great place to grow as an engineer. You get to work on large-scale, real enterprise systems used by major customers around the world, which is both challenging and rewarding. The engineering standards are high, and thereâs strong emphasis on code quality, security, and reliability. Workâlife balance is generally very good, with flexibility around working hours and remote work. The people are supportive, professional, and collaborative, and thereâs a strong culture of knowledge sharing. There are many opportunities to learn new technologies, move between teams, and build a long-term career within the company. Benefits are solid and management usually encourages personal development and training.
cons: Because SAP is a big organization, processes can be slow and sometimes overly bureaucratic. Decision-making may involve many stakeholders, which can delay implementation and reduce agility. Innovation exists, but it can take time for new ideas to move from concept to production. Some legacy systems and technical debt are still part of daily work in certain teams. Career progression can sometimes feel slower compared to startups, and promotions may depend more on organizational timing than purely on performance.

title: Great place to work, nice people, good bens / comp
body: Great place to work, nice people, good bens / comp
pros: - Compensation is competitive with the majority of the market - Very strong emphasis on a sustainable strategy for the future of ERP - Focus on celebrating success and employee experience is the best I've seen in a firm. - Strong but empathetic UK leaders / senior managers
cons: - The busy periods are very busy (particularly end of year). Although it's tough, if you put the effort in and do well, it's really noticed. - Main con is this focus on sustainable strategy has led to several rounds of redundancies across the global and UK organisation. It's concerning that employees are told 'that's it, no more reductions' and the next year, there's another announcement. Hoping this stops soon and the fear of job security will dissipate. - 3 days a week office announcements were a shock, just after closing many satellite offices more locally.
role: Current employee - Senior Service Delivery Manager

title: Good Overall
pros: There are still great leader at SAP that make your time truly valuable. The base pay is great and if you get lucky, you could make great money. Especially with the deal sizes of SAP. Very rare to encounter in the software industry. The company also invests heavily in its people. The culture is built around becoming the best version of yourself. You truly walk out of SAP with world class skills. Especially in sales.
cons: Quotas are extremely hard to achieve, and the comp plan is often tied to metrics that arenât fully in your control â like churn. For example, if your quota is $1M and you inherit an account that churns $1M, with only a $200K churn allowance, youâd need to sell $1.8M just to get to 100%. And honestly, thereâs no real work-life balance in this job. The hours are long, weekends are common, and it feels like thereâs less and less life balance at this company.

title: Toxic Management
pros: Work culture and company benifits on food, travel etc.
cons: I'm not sure if it's a general team dynamic or a specific issue with management, but I found the manager and senior team members to have a somewhat toxic or high level of intensity. Communication has been challenging at times, with managers sometimes yelling or expressing disapproval in front of junior developers. This approach can make the work environment feel less supportive and discourages open communication. I've noticed a tendency from management to prioritize their own needs and schedules above those of junior team members, leading to expectations that feel overly demanding or requiring excessive work hours. These observations create a sense that management may not fully value the contributions of junior team members or prioritize their well-being. It's possible that my experience is unique to this specific role or team, and my perception may not be representative

title: Great Benefits w/ Decent Pay, but limited Growth Opportunities
body: Great Benefits w/ Decent Pay, but limited Growth Opportunities
pros: Great for those looking to settle down for career stability, work-life balance, have families, or looking to "jumpstart" career in tech. Better opportunities if you prefer more managerial roles. - Decent base pay for amount of work, with great non-office benefits - Good Work-life balance due to European Culture - Relatively great stability (in terms of turnover) vs. similar-sized companies - Working hours not very strict, depending on manager.
cons: Not so great for those prioritizing super competitive pay, quick career progression, or high-impact and fast-pace jobs. Especially true if you're more of a technical track person. - While pay is decent, not as competitive vs. others w/ similar market cap/size, or other large tech companies. - Jobs feel relatively low impact if you're working in non-mainline or revenue-generating products. - Heavy bureaucracy due to company size, management decisions can be slow. - Technical/non-managerial positions experience slow career progression & restricted opportunities. Feels like it takes very long time to move up the rank ladder if you're not in management track. - Majority of bonus pay is based on company performance rather than individual contribution, so not super incentivized for growth/work. - Low visibility/understanding of company's overall objectives, making purpose of projects sometimes feel very confusing or direction-less

If you are seeking a high-tech, technically driven environment, this may not be the right place.

title: SAP
pros: Working directly for SAP grants you unrivaled access to internal product engineering and future roadmaps, allowing you to deliver authoritative solutions that external partners cannot match. Additionally, the strong brand reputation ensures you lead high-impact digital transformation projects for the worldâs largest top-tier clients.
cons: Navigating the massive organizational structure often involves complex bureaucracy and internal politics, which can slow down decision-making and resource allocation. Furthermore, there is intense pressure to balance high billable utilization with presales support to drive software revenue, often leading to a challenging work-life balance.

Instead of collaboration, people compete internally, undermining each other to get ahead. *The company hides behind flashy marketing and âcool brandingâ, but the reality inside is completely different â rotten to the core. * Salaries are mediocre. Frugality is the guiding principle of the whole company. *Customer satisfaction is not a priority. Most of the time they don't comeback after initial interest. *Leadership seems more interested in appearances and co-circular activities than in building a sustainable business or treating employees and customers with respect.

title: Sinking ship: poor culture, lack of innovation
body: Sinking ship: poor culture, lack of innovation
pros: Legacy benefits are good but I would not be surprised if those start rolling back as well. The individual contributors who do the work are great, and most people have a "we're in this together" mentality.
cons: Rolling quarterly layoffs, removal of RSU programs, less-than-industry-standard pay, senior leadership is nothing but hot air. There is no space for innovation, so our products are dinosaurs now- and going extinct as our competitors do things better.

title: Loved working here at one timeâ¦
body: Loved working here at one timeâ¦
pros: Great network of smart, clever people. Innovative products with a fantastic leader as SF Product head. Some really smart, much needed collaborations and partnerships recently announced.
cons: Loose focus of the customer at times making a CSM role more difficult than it should be. Internal policies are cumbersome and clouded making a CSM role about chasing help vs providing customers support. A lot of uncertainty across all LoBs and roles can make for a very chaotic and toxic working environment even though lower level leaders attempt to limit the disruption. You have to be a strong, independent worker to drown out the noise.

title: Slow-Moving Stability
pros: - Excellent pay - Annual quota instead of monthly - Realistic quota - Very low turnover - Slow to change which means a consistent and stable work environment
cons: - 2 mandatory call blocks daily (1 hour each) - 3 days/ week in office 8am-5pm Monday-Wednesday with no flexibility. Only the sales org is expected to come in at 8am, while others aren't in until 9am or later. - Mandatory 24 months in seat before promotion with no flexibility. This is not stated in the interview process, and the written contract is 12 months, but they will block you from moving to another role if you haven't been in your position for at least 24 months. Does not matter how much prior experience you have. - Limited PTO compared to other orgs (15 PTO + 5 personal)

title: Great people, but the bureaucracy is exhausting
body: Great people, but the bureaucracy is exhausting
pros: Many smart, dedicated, and collaborative colleagues. A strong industry presence. Benefits are competitive, and most teams try to support one another despite the challenges.
cons: Lack of clarity: Itâs often unclear whatâs actually expected in your role or for specific projects. Priorities shift frequently without a clear explanation of why or how success will be measured. Micromanagement from the top: The CEO and some board members involve themselves in day-to-day tactics and granular details that should be handled by the teams directly responsible. This slows progress and erodes autonomy. Overly complex processes: There is a process for absolutely everything, but they are unnecessarily complicated, outdated, and often conflicting. No guidance in navigating bureaucracy: While processes abound, there is little to no support in understanding how to work within them. Employees are left to figure it out on their own. Leadership avoids addressing the root issues: Instead of simplifying or fixing these systemic problems, top leaders push harder for results without acknowledging the inefficiencies and roadblocks theyâve created.

title: Once great culture has been decimated
body: Once great culture has been decimated
pros: The vast majority of colleagues are skilled, highly collaborative, and strive to make the company successful. Salary and benefits are decent.
cons: I was proud to work for SAP for a long time, but the culture in recent years has shifted to an exclusive focus on maximizing the stock price and reducing financial rewards (e.g., RSUs), with little regard for employee morale. While the US is the largest market, there have been mass layoffs here each year, focused on older workers and higher salaries. Replacement roles are at lower salary bands and are usually backfilled in India. Power and decision-making have become highly centralized in Germany, with strategy changing each year to follow the latest advice from McKinsey.

body: Supportive Culture with Internal Growth â But Slow to Change

Internal events foster collaboration and connection, and there are numerous opportunities to grow into diverse roles. cons: The company is less competitive compared to U.S. software firms, especially in terms of pace and mindset. title: Company has taken a horrible turn for sales
body: Company has taken a horrible turn for sales
pros: Flexible work from home. Generally smart people at least in the sales department.. unless they all leave after this year.
cons: After several years in sales at SAP - this used to be one of the top companies in big tech to work for. In effort of slashing the cost of sales, they have completely eliminated the perks and compensation for being in sales at SAP. Trying to figure out how commissions works is a full time job and science project. Trying to hold SAP accountable for actually paying commissions in full has become impossible. Quotas have doubled while territories shrunk, accelerators removed, and other BS policies like holding back 30% of commissions because of adoption factors which are always out of your control. This year, I expect to make less than half the total cash than what i used to make at SAP, and I don't see that changing in '26. Our best people are leaving. Our recent internal employee satisfaction survey scored as low as 1/10 or 2/10 in many areas such as "would you recommend SAP as a place to work".

title: Former RISE-EA
pros: My time at SAP was incredibly rewardingâboth professionally and personally. I had the opportunity to work alongside global executive leaders on high-impact transformation initiatives, gaining firsthand experience in aligning people, process, and technology at scale. I sharpened my skills in organizational change management, executive communications, and stakeholder engagement while contributing to enterprise-wide strategy and cultural transformation. One of the biggest highlights was supporting board-level communications and leading internal alignment efforts across HR, product, and customer success. SAPâs fast-paced, matrixed environment challenged me to be both strategic and agile, and it truly strengthened my ability to drive change in complex, global organizations.
cons: One of the challenges during my time at SAP was navigating multiple organizational restructures. While change is part of any large enterprise, these shifts often led to talent transitions and loss of institutional knowledge across key teams. This impacted continuity, slowed momentum on long-term initiatives, and sometimes required re-establishing stakeholder alignment from the ground up. It reinforced for me the importance of strong knowledge transfer practices, stakeholder mapping, and change readiness planningâespecially in dynamic environments. These experiences deepened my ability to lead through uncertainty and helped me develop strategies to maintain engagement and clarity amidst evolving priorities.

title: Avoid SAP CX/FLEX Team â Toxic Management Culture
body: Avoid SAP CX/FLEX Team â Toxic Management Culture
pros: The team is led by a newly promoted female manager who lacks the leadership maturity and people skills needed for such a role. Favors a personal circle, creating an exclusionary environment where decisions are influenced more by personal bias than team merit. Open feedback is discouraged, and any form of constructive suggestion is treated defensively. Several good team members have quietly left due to the poor culture, but the reasons are often masked as "location changes" to avoid attention. The work atmosphere feels tense, gossipy, and politically driven â not collaborative or growth-oriented.
cons: Recently promoted manager lacks basic team management and people handling skills. Displays controlling behavior and does not listen to team input. Favors a select group of individuals, creating an unhealthy team culture rooted in gossip and exclusion. Innovation and initiative are not encouraged â dissenting voices often get sidelined. High attrition rate within the team, with multiple exits within short spans.

cons: All downsides of a big organisation - politics, siloed orgs, internal competition and putting own org priorities ahead of the company's. Radical structural changes in 2025 are causing chaos distress and inefficiency among lack of motivation. It seems new hires are more valued (in terms of salary) compared to already hired employees. Tendence for open-space office setup with employees fighting to have walls and doors installed (to deal with office noise). Few teams are driving actually strategic for the company topics.

pros: Amazing people, great place to grow especially if you are starting out in your career.
cons: You gotta work early hours as you'll be doing lots of meetings with German colleagues. Office culture in Vancouver is not the same as it was years ago. There's a stronger culture in the other Canadian locations.
